fact: June 13 sits exactly 164 days into the year, which means there are exactly 201 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this date typically falls in the middle of peak firefly season - millions of glowing bugs light up backyards like tiny disco balls!
The sun rises before 5:30 AM in most northern cities on June 13, making it one of the earliest sunrise days of the entire year.
June 13 often marks the time when strawberry plants in temperate regions are bursting with the juiciest, reddest berries - nature's candy at its peak!
In Antarctica, June 13 brings some of the coldest, darkest days of winter with temperatures that can freeze your spit before it hits the ground.
This date falls during Gemini season, when the constellation of the twins is positioned in the night sky for stargazing.
June 13 happens exactly one week before the summer solstice, meaning daylight is still getting longer by about 30 seconds each day in the Northern Hemisphere.
Mosquitoes typically reach their most annoying peak around June 13 in many regions - female mosquitoes are hunting for blood to fuel their egg-laying frenzy.
The moon phase on June 13 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it creates some of the brightest summer nights possible.
In northern forests, June 13 often marks when many baby animals like deer fawns and fox kits are taking their very first wobbly steps outside their dens.
Ocean water temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere are warming rapidly around June 13, making it prime time for jellyfish blooms to appear in coastal waters.
June 13 falls during the season when Earth is actually moving fastest in its orbit around the sun, zooming through space at over 67,000 miles per hour!
